planting.note: Set out this female vine in early spring with a male pollinator nearby.

directions: A popular productive female hardy kiwi whose grape-sized green fruit has a pineapple-like aroma, requiring a male arguta vine for pollination. Prune hard in late winter and again in summer to keep the rampant growth in check and expose fruit to sun. Pick the smooth-skinned berries in early autumn when they yield to gentle pressure.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; a single self-fertile vine can set fruit alone.

directions: This self-fertile hardy kiwi is prized for small gardens because it can fruit without a separate male, though a pollinator boosts yield. It is less rampant than most arguta vines and often bears in only two or three years. Harvest the smooth green fruit in early fall when soft and sweet.

planting.note: Plant this female vine in early spring alongside a male pollinator.

directions: A female hardy kiwi bearing sweet fruit that flushes reddish-purple where sun strikes the skin, needing a male arguta for pollination. Grow on a strong trellis and prune in winter to renew fruiting wood. Fruit colors and softens in autumn and is eaten whole like a grape.

planting.note: Set out in early spring with a compatible male vine for fruit set.

directions: An early-ripening female hardy kiwi with sweet honey-flavored fruit, dependent on a nearby male for pollination. Give it a substantial support and prune to keep the vigorous canes manageable. Pick the smooth green fruit in late summer to early fall before autumn frosts.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; grow male and female Weiki vines together for fruit.

directions: This hardy German selection is sold as separate male and female vines that must be paired to set the sweet grape-sized fruit. It tolerates hard winters and grows vigorously, so an ample arbor and yearly pruning are essential. Harvest the smooth berries in early autumn once they soften.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; pair a variegated male with a female for fruit.

directions: The hardiest of the edible kiwis, valued as much for its pink-and-white splashed male foliage as for its small sweet fruit. Give it some afternoon shade in hot climates to protect the leaf color and a modest trellis to climb. Females bear tiny grape-like fruit in late summer when a male vine is present.

planting.note: Plant dormant vines in early spring in a spot with light shade.

directions: An extremely cold-tolerant kiwi species with smaller, sweeter fruit and striking variegated foliage on male plants. It is less rampant than other kiwis and does well on a fence or small arbor with some shade in warm regions. Plant both sexes for a light crop of tiny smooth-skinned fruit in late summer.

planting.note: Plant outdoors only after all frost has passed and soil has warmed.

directions: Sow scarified seed about a quarter inch deep in warm soil, or set out a rooted cutting after frost, and give the vine a fence or trellis to climb. Water and feed regularly through the warm season and protect from cold, as vines are damaged below freezing. The purple fruit is ripe when it wrinkles and drops, with fragrant, tart-sweet pulp inside.

planting.note: Set out in late spring once nights stay reliably warm.

directions: This tropical yellow-fruited form is more vigorous and heat-loving than the purple type but even less cold-tolerant. Many yellow vines need cross-pollination or hand pollination to set a heavy crop of large tart fruit. Harvest when the golden fruit begins to wrinkle and falls, then scoop the aromatic pulp.

planting.note: Plant rooted vines after the last frost in warm, well-drained soil.

directions: A reliably self-fertile named variety producing large purple-blushed fruit with tart-sweet pulp, popular for home gardens. Train on a strong trellis, feed through the growing season, and shelter from frost. The heavy fruit is ready when it wrinkles slightly and falls to the ground.

planting.note: Plant after frost; this grafted selection tolerates a little more cold than seedlings.

directions: A widely grown, hardy and productive purple passionfruit usually sold as a grafted plant for extra vigor and cold tolerance. Give it a sunny fence, steady water, and regular feeding to carry a large crop. Harvest the round purple fruit once the skin dimples and it drops of its own accord.

planting.note: Set out rooted vines in late spring after the soil warms.

directions: A self-fertile purple variety known for large, flavorful fruit and dependable cropping in warm regions. Provide a robust trellis, rich soil, and consistent moisture to sustain the fast-growing vine. Pick the deep purple fruit when it softens and wrinkles for the sweetest pulp.

planting.note: Plant after frost; tolerates cooler highland conditions than most passionfruits.

directions: This vigorous vine bears long pink pendulous flowers followed by elongated yellow banana-shaped fruit with tangy pulp. It grows well in cool, mild climates but can become weedy, so manage its spread carefully. Pick the soft yellow fruit when it colors fully and yields to a gentle squeeze.

planting.note: Set out dormant plants in early spring once hard freezes have passed.

directions: Root a dormant hardwood cutting about six inches deep, or plant a young tree with its roots spread in a wide hole. Water steadily through the first summer and mulch to keep the shallow roots cool and moist. Pick figs only when they hang soft and droop on the stem, as they will not sweeten further off the tree.

planting.note: Vigorous and large; give it room and full sun in mild-winter regions.

directions: Give this large grower plenty of space and prune only to shape, as it fruits on both old and new wood. The deep purple-black figs have rich, sweet amber flesh good fresh or dried. Harvest a light spring crop and a larger main crop in late summer.

planting.note: Large-leaved and cold-tolerant; good for cooler fig regions.

directions: Plant in a sheltered, sunny position where the large deeply-lobed leaves are protected from wind. It bears an early breba crop of very large figs plus a main crop. Pick the elongated brown-purple fruit when fully soft and richly colored.

planting.note: Reliable breba crop makes it ideal for cool, short-summer climates.

directions: Choose this variety in cool regions because it ripens a heavy early breba crop before summer ends. Prune lightly, since hard pruning removes the overwintered wood that carries the main crop. Harvest the green-skinned figs with deep strawberry flesh when they hang soft.

planting.note: Good producer in cooler coastal and Pacific Northwest gardens.

directions: Plant in full sun in a spot that captures reflected warmth in mild-summer areas. It reliably ripens even where heat is limited, making it popular along the coast. Harvest the dark reddish-brown figs with sweet amber flesh once they soften.

planting.note: Needs a long warm season to ripen its striped fruit fully.

directions: Give this ornamental variety the warmest spot so its late crop can finish ripening. The green-and-yellow striped figs turn showy as they mature and hold strawberry-red flesh. Harvest when the stripes soften and the fruit droops, typically late in the season.

planting.note: An old pale-fruited variety that ripens in cooler summers.

directions: Plant this heritage fig in a warm, sheltered position such as against a sunny wall. It bears sweet, almost translucent pale-green figs and can crop well even in mild climates. Harvest when the greenish-yellow skin softens and the honeyed flesh yields to a gentle squeeze.

planting.note: Vigorous variety valued for jam and drying; likes summer heat.

directions: Allow room for this vigorous tree and prune to keep fruit within reach. The green-skinned figs hide bright strawberry-pink flesh prized for preserves. Harvest when the skin softens and cracks slightly, usually in the heat of late summer.

planting.note: Heat-loving and disease resistant; good fresh or dried.

directions: Plant in a hot, sunny location where its resistance to splitting and souring pays off. This vigorous tree bears heavy crops of pale figs with sweet white-to-pink flesh. Harvest when the greenish-white fruit softens; it dries well on the tree in dry heat.

planting.note: A sweet yellow Kadota-type; productive in warm gardens.

directions: Site this variety in full sun and water evenly during fruit swell. It produces sweet, mild yellow figs that resist cracking better than many light types. Harvest when the fruit softens and the amber flesh becomes richly sweet.

planting.note: Long fruiting season with a closed eye that resists spoilage.

directions: Plant in full sun and keep it evenly watered to sustain its long crop. The brownish-purple striped figs have a tight eye that keeps out moisture and insects. Harvest over an extended late-season window as the fruit softens and sweetens.

planting.note: Naturally small tree that fruits young; good for containers.

directions: Its compact habit suits a pot or small yard, and it begins bearing at a young age. Prune to keep it tidy and water steadily during fruiting. Harvest the small greenish-yellow figs with sweet pink flesh when they soften on the branch.

planting.note: Very sweet yellow fig; give it the warmest spot to ripen.

directions: Plant against a warm wall or in a sheltered sunny corner to gather heat. The lemon-yellow figs are exceptionally sweet with deep amber flesh. Harvest when the fruit turns soft and its honeyed juice begins to bead at the eye.

planting.note: Hardy honey fig that ripens in cool Northern summers.

directions: A good choice for cooler regions, it bears both an early and a main crop of pale figs. Plant in full sun and mulch well to protect the roots in winter. Harvest the greenish-yellow figs when they soften and their thick syrupy flesh sweetens.

planting.note: Productive over a long season and quick to recover from cold.

directions: Plant in full sun and it will bear a long, steady crop through the warm months. It resprouts and fruits quickly even after a hard winter cuts it back. Harvest the medium mahogany-brown figs with strawberry flesh as they soften.

planting.note: Early-ripening and cold-tolerant; excellent for short seasons.

directions: This variety ripens very early, making it ideal for cooler or shorter-season gardens. Plant in a warm sunny spot and prune to keep it compact. Harvest the small round dark-purple figs when they hang soft and split slightly at the eye.

planting.note: One of the earliest figs; well suited to cold, short summers.

directions: Choose this fig where seasons are short, as it ripens its main crop remarkably early. Plant in full sun and it will fruit even in cool climates. Harvest the small yellow-green to brownish figs with amber flesh as soon as they soften.

planting.note: Late-ripening gourmet fig needing a long, hot season.

directions: Give this prized Catalan fig the hottest, sunniest spot to finish its late crop. Patience is rewarded with dense, richly flavored dark figs. Harvest the flattened black-purple fruit with deep red flesh once fully soft in autumn.

planting.note: Very large fruit with a distinctive elongated neck.

directions: Plant in full sun and water generously to swell its unusually large, long-necked figs. Thin fruit if the tree overbears so the remaining figs size up. Harvest the pale-yellow fruit with light amber flesh when it softens and the neck bends.

planting.note: Pale-fruited variety that performs in cooler coastal gardens.

directions: Plant in full sun in a sheltered spot, as it ripens well even in mild-summer areas. It bears an early breba crop plus a main crop of pale figs. Harvest the greenish-yellow fruit with sweet amber-pink flesh once it softens.

planting.note: Long-season yellow fig with a tight eye and few seeds.

directions: Plant in full sun where a long warm season lets its heavy crop ripen fully. The closed eye helps the pale figs resist spoilage in humidity. Harvest the yellow-green fruit with sweet syrupy flesh over an extended late season.

planting.note: Rich dark fig once grown at Thomas Jefferson's Monticello.

directions: Plant this heritage fig in full sun and prune to keep the small tree productive. It bears intensely sweet dark figs on a compact frame. Harvest the small black-purple fruit with red flesh when it softens and droops.

planting.note: Early French fig bearing two heavy crops in warm regions.

directions: Plant in full sun where it will reward you with an early breba crop and a generous main crop. Keep it evenly watered through fruit set for the best size and flavor. Harvest the small gray-brown figs with sweet amber-red flesh as they soften.

planting.note: Gourmet French fig that needs a long, hot season to ripen.

directions: Reserve the hottest, sunniest position for this celebrated dessert fig. It ripens somewhat late but delivers exceptional flavor when fully mature. Harvest the round purple-gray figs with deep red flesh once they turn soft.

planting.note: Larger-fruited Celeste selection with the same closed eye.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil and full sun, avoiding heavy feeding that delays fruiting. It keeps Celeste's spoilage-resistant closed eye while yielding larger figs. Harvest the purple-brown fruit with sweet amber flesh when it softens in summer.

planting.note: Cold-tolerant Brown Turkey strain from the Pacific Northwest.

directions: This hardy selection ripens reliably in cooler climates and resprouts well after cold. Plant in full sun and cut back to maintain a manageable size. Harvest the brown-purple figs with amber flesh when they soften and their necks bend.

planting.note: Very large green figs that ripen in cool-summer regions.

directions: Plant in full sun and water steadily to develop its very large fruit. It ripens well even where summers are mild, making it useful in cooler gardens. Harvest the big green-skinned figs with sweet strawberry-red flesh once they soften.

planting.note: Dark French fig with a long, productive main crop.

directions: Plant in full sun and keep it watered for a long, steady run of dark figs. The elongated fruit carries sweet, richly colored flesh. Harvest the purple-black figs when they soften and hang heavily on the stem.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring after the last frost when soil has warmed.

directions: Set a grafted tree with the graft union above the soil and mulch the root zone while keeping mulch off the trunk. This prized Indian dessert mango carries fiberless saffron flesh and needs full sun with a dry spell before bloom. Harvest in early summer when the fruit turns golden and gives off a rich, sweet perfume.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring once nights stay reliably warm.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained ground and water deeply while the tree establishes. This vigorous, disease-tolerant Florida cultivar produces firm, long-keeping fruit with a thick red-and-green skin that ships well. Pick when the green ground color lightens and the blush deepens; the flesh has a mild flavor and some fiber near the seed.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring after the frost-free date.

directions: Give this Florida selection full sun and a deep, well-drained soil, watering steadily as fruit swells. Kent bears large, greenish-yellow fruit with sweet, nearly fiberless flesh that is excellent fresh or dried. Harvest in mid to late season when a yellow blush spreads over the shoulders and the fruit yields slightly to gentle pressure.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in warm spring soil.

directions: Plant in full sun with good drainage and keep the tree evenly watered through the long fruiting period. Keitt is a late-season Florida cultivar bearing very large fruit that often stays green even when ripe, with sweet, fiberless flesh. Harvest when the fruit softens slightly and a faint yellow tinge appears rather than waiting for full color change.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring after frost.

directions: Set this classic Florida tree in full sun and rich, well-drained soil, staking it while young. Haden bears aromatic fruit with bright red blush and rich, slightly fibrous flesh and is the parent of many modern cultivars. Harvest in early to mid season when the crimson shoulders and yellow ground color develop and the aroma sweetens.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring; suits container growing in marginal areas.

directions: This Thai favorite grows to a manageable size and takes well to pruning or a large pot in full sun. It bears elongated, S-shaped fruit with intensely sweet, fiberless flesh that many enjoy eaten green as well as ripe. Harvest when the pale green skin brightens toward yellow and the fruit softens near the tip.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring after the last frost.

directions: Grow this Mexican cultivar, also sold as Honey or Champagne mango, in full sun with steady moisture as fruit forms. The small, kidney-shaped fruit has buttery, fiberless golden flesh and a thin skin that wrinkles slightly when fully ripe. Harvest when the skin turns deep yellow and the flesh softens to a rich, sweet texture.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring once soil warms.

directions: This Australian standard, also called Bowen, wants full sun and free-draining soil with deep watering during fruit development. It carries aromatic fruit with a yellow-orange blush and sweet, slightly fibrous flesh well suited to fresh eating. Harvest when the ground color turns yellow and the characteristic mango fragrance is strong.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in warm spring weather.

directions: Plant this Philippine cultivar in full sun with excellent drainage and water regularly while establishing. It bears slender, kidney-shaped fruit with thin skin and sweet, fiberless yellow flesh prized for fresh eating and drying. Harvest when the green skin turns fully yellow and the fruit gives gently to the touch.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring after frost.

directions: Give this late-season Florida cultivar full sun and deep, well-drained soil. Palmer produces long, oval fruit with a purple-red blush and firm, nearly fiberless flesh, and the tree tends to bear reliably. Harvest when the ground color yellows and the fruit softens slightly at the stem end.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring once frost has passed.

directions: This vigorous Haden seedling wants full sun and room to grow tall, with deep watering as fruit fills. It bears long, slender fruit with a bright yellow-and-red skin and sweet, fiberless flesh with a rich aroma. Harvest in mid to late season when the color brightens and the fruit yields to light pressure.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring; a good choice for smaller yards.

directions: This mild-flavored Florida cultivar keeps a moderate size and grows well in full sun with regular water. Glenn bears sweet, fiberless fruit with a peachy aroma and a colorful yellow-orange blush, and it is a dependable early-season producer. Harvest when the ground color turns yellow and the fruit softens slightly.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring after the frost-free date.

directions: Grow this compact Florida cultivar in full sun with well-drained soil and even moisture. Irwin bears attractive crimson fruit with sweet, fiberless flesh and is popular in Japan and Taiwan where trees are often grown under cover. Harvest when the skin flushes bright red over yellow and the fruit gives gently.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring; a natural dwarf suited to containers.

directions: This dwarf Caribbean cultivar stays small enough for a large pot and does best in full sun with steady moisture. Julie bears flat, plump fruit with rich, spicy-sweet flesh that has a soft, melting texture, though the tree can be prone to disease in very humid sites. Harvest when the skin blushes yellow and pink and the fruit softens.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring after frost.

directions: This Haitian cultivar, sometimes spelled Francine, grows in full sun with well-drained soil and regular water. It bears slender, S-curved fruit with soft, rich, fiberless flesh and a bright yellow skin flecked with green. Harvest when the green streaks fade toward full yellow and the fruit softens and sweetens.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring once soil warms.

directions: Grow this classic North Indian cultivar in full sun with deep, well-drained soil and a dry period before flowering. Dasheri bears small to medium fruit with sweet, aromatic, fiberless flesh and a pale green-yellow skin. Harvest in early summer when the fruit softens slightly and the fragrance intensifies.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring after frost.

directions: This well-known North Indian cultivar prefers full sun and free-draining soil with a dry spell to trigger bloom. Langra keeps a greenish skin even when ripe and offers rich, aromatic, fiber-free flesh. Harvest in mid summer when the fruit softens and its perfume grows strong despite the persistent green color.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in warm spring weather.

directions: This celebrated Pakistani cultivar wants full sun, deep soil, and a dry period before flowering for a heavy set. Chaunsa bears golden-yellow fruit with very sweet, aromatic, nearly fiberless flesh. Harvest in mid to late summer when the skin turns rich yellow and the fruit softens and smells fragrant.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring after the last frost.

directions: Grow this large-fruited Pakistani cultivar from Sindh in full sun with excellent drainage. Sindhri produces big, oval fruit with sweet, aromatic, fiberless flesh and a smooth yellow skin. Harvest in early to mid summer when the green ground color lightens to yellow and the fruit yields to gentle pressure.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring once soil warms.

directions: This major South Indian cultivar, also called Baneshan, grows in full sun with well-drained soil and a dry season before bloom. It bears large, oblong yellow fruit with firm, sweet, fiberless flesh and a thin skin. Harvest when the skin turns golden yellow and the fruit gives slightly at the stem end.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring; a compact regular bearer.

directions: This late-season South Indian cultivar stays fairly compact and bears more regularly than many mangoes. Grow in full sun with good drainage and steady water as fruit fills. Neelum offers small to medium fruit with sweet, aromatic, fiberless flesh; harvest when the skin turns yellow-orange and the fragrance strengthens.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring after frost.

directions: Grow this South Indian processing cultivar in full sun with well-drained soil. Totapuri bears distinctive beaked, oblong fruit with firm, mildly sweet and slightly tart flesh often used for juicing and pickling. Harvest when the skin turns golden yellow; the flesh stays firm and holds up well for cooking.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring; stays modest and suits pruning.

directions: This Indian hybrid of Neelum and Dasheri keeps a manageable size and does well in full sun with regular water. Mallika bears rich, fiberless fruit with a honeyed, citrus-melon flavor and deep orange flesh. Harvest when the skin turns yellow and the fruit gives off a strong, sweet aroma.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring; a dwarf hybrid good for close planting.

directions: This dwarf Indian hybrid of Dasheri and Neelum suits high-density planting and container culture in full sun. Amrapali is a regular, heavy bearer of small, deep-orange, fiberless fruit with rich, sweet flesh. Harvest in the late season when the skin turns yellow-orange and the fruit softens slightly.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring after frost.

directions: This Florida-origin cultivar is widely grown in Spain and wants full sun with well-drained soil. Osteen bears oval fruit with a purple-and-orange skin and mild, sweet, low-fiber flesh that stores and ships well. Harvest when the ground color yellows beneath the blush and the fruit softens gently.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in warm spring soil.

directions: This Thai cultivar grows to a moderate size and thrives in full sun with steady water during fruiting. Mahachanok bears elongated fruit with an attractive yellow-and-red blush and sweet, fiberless, aromatic flesh. Harvest when the colors brighten and the fruit yields slightly to gentle pressure.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring once frost risk ends.

directions: This Australian cultivar, pronounced arr-two-ee-two, grows vigorously in full sun with well-drained soil. R2E2 bears very large, round fruit with an orange-red blush and firm, sweet, low-fiber flesh that keeps and ships well. Harvest when the ground color turns yellow and the fruit begins to soften.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring; an excellent condo or container mango.

directions: This Florida dwarf keeps naturally small and responds well to pruning for pots or tight spaces in full sun. Cogshall bears fiber-free fruit with sweet, aromatic flesh and a colorful red-and-yellow skin. Harvest when the blush deepens and the ground color yellows and the fruit softens slightly.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring; a compact bushy dwarf for containers.

directions: This compact, bushy Florida dwarf is ideal for large pots and small yards in full sun. Pickering bears sweet, fiberless fruit with a coconut-like richness on a tree that seldom outgrows its space. Harvest when the yellow ground color develops under the pink blush and the fruit yields gently.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring; an early-season variety.

directions: This Florida cultivar is prized as one of the earliest mangoes to ripen each season and grows well in full sun. Rosigold bears small to medium fruit with sweet, rich, fiberless flesh and a yellow-orange skin. Harvest in early season when the color deepens and the fruit softens and turns fragrant.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring; a manageable modern selection.

directions: This modern Zill selection from Florida grows to a moderate size and does best in full sun with even moisture. Lemon Zest bears rich, fiberless fruit noted for a distinct citrus tang layered over classic mango sweetness. Harvest when the skin turns golden yellow and the fruit softens and its lemony aroma develops.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring; compact and container-friendly.

directions: This compact Zill cultivar suits pots and smaller gardens and grows best in full sun with steady water. Coconut Cream bears fiberless fruit famous for its rich, tropical flavor reminiscent of coconut and pina colada. Harvest when the greenish-yellow skin softens and the sweet aroma builds.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring after frost.

directions: This Zill selection grows to a moderate size and wants full sun with well-drained soil. Sweet Tart bears fiberless fruit with a bold balance of high sweetness and bright acidity that gives it a complex, tangy flavor. Harvest when the skin turns yellow with a blush and the fruit gives to light pressure.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring; a reliable dwarf for humid areas.

directions: This dwarf cultivar from the Panama Canal Zone tolerates humid conditions and stays small enough for containers in full sun. Fairchild bears sweet, fiberless fruit with a mild, pleasant flavor and is a dependable bearer. Harvest when the yellow skin develops and the fruit softens slightly.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring after frost.

directions: This Florida cultivar, a Haden and Carabao cross, grows to a moderate size in full sun with good drainage. Edward is regarded as one of the finest-flavored mangoes, bearing sweet, aromatic, fiberless fruit, though it can set lightly. Harvest when the skin flushes yellow with a pink blush and the fruit gives to gentle pressure.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring after the last frost.

directions: This slender-fruited cultivar widely grown in Mexico wants full sun and free-draining soil with regular water. Manila bears small, elongated fruit with thin skin and sweet, fiberless yellow flesh well suited to fresh eating. Harvest when the green skin turns fully yellow-orange and the fruit softens.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring once soil warms.

directions: This Florida cultivar stays moderately sized and grows well in full sun with good drainage. Van Dyke bears small, colorful fruit with a bright red-orange skin and sweet, mildly spicy, low-fiber flesh. Harvest when the blush deepens and the ground color yellows and the fruit softens gently.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring after frost.

directions: This Florida cultivar is valued for its disease resistance and grows well in humid climates in full sun. Duncan bears fiberless fruit with firm, sweet flesh on a consistently productive tree. Harvest when the skin turns yellow and the fruit begins to soften and release its aroma.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring; a compact disease-tolerant dwarf.

directions: This compact Florida cultivar tolerates disease well and suits containers or small gardens in full sun. Carrie bears fiberless fruit with rich, sweet, aromatic flesh that many rank among the best-flavored mangoes. The skin stays green when ripe, so harvest when the fruit softens and gives off a strong fragrance.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring after frost.

directions: This late-season Florida cultivar keeps a moderate size and grows in full sun with well-drained soil. Sensation bears small, deep red fruit with firm, mildly sweet, low-fiber flesh and holds its dark color attractively. Harvest when the ground color under the red skin yellows and the fruit softens slightly.

planting.note: Plant after the last frost when nights stay reliably warm.

directions: Plant a healthy pup with its corm just under the soil in a wind-sheltered, sunny spot. Water generously and mulch thickly to hold moisture, and support the heavy bunch as it fills out. Harvest when the fruit is full and light green, before it yellows on the plant.

planting.note: Set out suckers in spring once the ground is thoroughly warm.

directions: Give this tall grower deep, fertile soil and plenty of room, planting the corm shallowly in a protected location. Feed and water consistently, removing extra suckers so a single strong stem carries the bunch. Cut the stalk when the fingers are rounded and full, and let them finish ripening in a warm, shaded place.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring after frost danger ends.

directions: Plant the corm of a sword sucker just below the surface in warm, well-drained soil. Water steadily and shelter from wind, since this tall type is prone to toppling under a full bunch. Harvest the small, sweet fingers when they are plump and pale green, ripening them indoors.

planting.note: Plant in spring; this cultivar tolerates a bit more cold than most.

directions: Set a rooted pup into rich, moisture-retentive soil in full sun with wind protection. Keep it well fed and watered through summer for a smooth, silvery-blue bunch. Harvest when the fruit loses its blue cast and rounds out, then ripen off the plant for its creamy, vanilla-like flavor.

planting.note: Plant after frost; this type wants a long, warm growing season.

directions: Plant the corm shallowly in deep, fertile soil and give it a sheltered spot to develop over its long season. Feed regularly and never let the soil dry, as the maroon-skinned fruit needs sustained warmth to sweeten. Cut the bunch when the red skin deepens and the fingers fill, ripening slowly to a soft, sweet flesh.

planting.note: Plant suckers in spring in a hot, sheltered position.

directions: Plant a rooted division into warm, rich soil and stake as the tall stem develops. Water and feed steadily so the short, chubby fingers can fill out. Let the fruit ripen fully to yellow flecked with black, which brings out its apple-and-strawberry aroma.

planting.note: Plant in spring; this vigorous, wind-tolerant type suits gustier gardens.

directions: Plant the corm just below grade in fertile soil in full sun. Its broad, sturdy leaves and stout trunk stand up to wind better than most, though it still appreciates rich feeding and steady water. Harvest the sweet, medium fingers when they round out and pale.

planting.note: Plant in spring; this hybrid is bred for disease resistance and vigor.

directions: Plant a rooted pup into warm, well-drained soil in full sun. This FHIA hybrid grows strongly and shrugs off many leaf diseases, but still rewards heavy feeding and consistent water. Harvest when the firm, apple-tart fruit fills out, using it either green for cooking or fully ripe fresh.

planting.note: Plant suckers in spring once frost has passed.

directions: Plant the corm shallowly in rich soil and give it a warm, sheltered position. The fingers fuse in pairs so the hands look like clasped hands, and the plant needs the usual generous feeding and watering. Harvest when the fused fingers round out, ripening to a mildly sweet, vanilla-scented fruit.

planting.note: Plant out in spring when nights are warm.

directions: Plant a rooted division with the corm just under the soil in full sun. This productive Cavendish-type carries a heavy bunch, so support the stalk and keep it fed and watered. Harvest when the fingers are full and pale green, ripening off the plant.

planting.note: Plant in spring; its compact size suits smaller gardens and containers.

directions: Plant the corm just below grade in rich, well-drained soil; the short stature makes it easy to shelter or grow in a large pot. Feed and water steadily for its heavy hands of sweet, creamy fruit. Harvest when the fingers plump up and lighten, then ripen indoors.

planting.note: Plant suckers in spring in a warm, sheltered site.

directions: Plant the corm shallowly in deep, fertile soil in full sun. This hardy, disease-tolerant type grows tall and bears a big bunch of small fingers, so give it room and steady feeding. Harvest when the thin-skinned fruit rounds out, ripening to a sweet, slightly tart flavor.

planting.note: Plant in spring; this vigorous cooking type needs a long, warm season.

directions: Give this large grower deep, fertile soil and ample space, setting the corm just below grade. It is a strong, drought-tolerant plant once established but produces best with rich feeding and steady water. Harvest the starchy, angular fruit while firm for boiling and frying, as it is used cooked rather than eaten raw.

planting.note: Plant in spring; its compact, sturdy habit handles wind and containers well.

directions: Plant the corm just under the soil in full sun in rich, well-drained ground. Its stout trunk and short stature stand up to wind and make it a good container subject with feeding and steady water. Harvest the sweet-tart fingers when they round out and pale.

planting.note: Plant suckers in spring in a hot, sheltered spot.

directions: Plant the corm shallowly in fertile soil in full sun. This Hawaiian type bears very large, plump fingers on a compact stem, so support the heavy bunch and feed generously. Use the starchy fruit fried or roasted when green, or let it ripen for a soft, sweet flesh.

planting.note: Plant in spring once the soil is warm.

directions: Plant a rooted pup with the corm just below grade in rich, sunny ground. Feed and water steadily for its orange-fleshed, aromatic fingers prized in Southeast Asia. Harvest when the fruit fills out and colors, ripening to a honeyed sweetness.

planting.note: Plant suckers in spring in a warm, protected location.

directions: Plant the corm shallowly in fertile soil; this Hawaiian variety shows striking black-green pseudostems and leaf stalks. Feed and water heavily through the warm season for its blocky, dual-purpose fruit. Use the fingers green for cooking or ripen them for fresh eating.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sheltered, sunny position.

directions: Plant the corm just under the soil in rich ground in full sun. This Pacific Islander bears short, fat fingers with pinkish-orange flesh on a compact plant, so keep it well fed and watered. Cook the starchy fruit when green, or let it ripen for a soft, mild flesh.

planting.note: Plant suckers in spring; plantains need a long, hot season to fill out.

directions: Plant the corm just below grade in deep, fertile, evenly moist soil in full sun. This true plantain carries a large bunch of many slender fingers, so feed heavily and support the stalk. Harvest the starchy fruit green for cooking, when the fingers are full and angular.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a hot, sheltered spot with rich soil.

directions: Plant the corm shallowly in deep, fertile soil in full sun and shelter from wind. This plantain bears fewer but very large, horn-shaped fingers, so keep it fed and watered through the season. Harvest the starchy fruit green for frying and boiling, when the fingers reach full size.

planting.note: Plant in spring; the compact habit suits smaller plots.

directions: Plant the corm just below the soil in rich ground in full sun. This shorter plantain fits smaller gardens while still yielding a full bunch of cooking fruit, so feed and water it steadily. Harvest the fingers green and starchy for frying, boiling, or mashing.

planting.note: Plant in spring; this hardy cooking type takes a long, warm season.

directions: Plant the corm just under the soil in fertile ground in full sun. This sturdy, drought-tolerant cooking banana bears angular, starchy fingers and stands up to rough conditions once established. Harvest while firm and green for boiling and frying.

planting.note: Plant suckers in spring in a warm, sheltered site.

directions: Plant the corm just below grade in rich, well-drained soil in full sun. This vigorous, disease-tolerant Indian variety bears dense hands of small, firm fingers, so feed and water it steadily. Harvest when the thin-skinned fruit rounds out, ripening to a sweet, slightly acid flavor.

planting.note: Plant in spring; the enormous bunch needs a long, warm season.

directions: Plant the corm shallowly in deep, fertile soil in full sun and shelter from wind. This curiosity forms an extremely long, drooping stalk crowded with hundreds of tiny fingers, so brace the heavy inflorescence. Harvest the small sweet fruit as the hands fill along the elongating stalk.

planting.note: Plant in spring; this hybrid is bred for disease resistance and heavy yield.

directions: Plant a rooted pup into warm, well-drained soil in full sun. This FHIA dessert-and-cooking hybrid resists Sigatoka leaf disease and carries a large bunch, so feed generously and support the stalk. Harvest the tart-sweet fruit when full; eat ripe or cook while still green.

planting.note: Plant in spring; the shorter habit is easier to shelter than tall reds.

directions: Plant the corm just under the soil in rich, sunny ground with wind protection. This compact red-skinned selection bears the sweet, maroon-fingered fruit on a shorter, more manageable stem. Harvest when the skin deepens to red and the fingers fill, ripening slowly to a soft, sweet flesh.

planting.note: Plant in spring; this dwarf is ideal for pots and small patios.

directions: Plant the corm just below the surface in a large container or warm border bed in full sun. Its very short stature makes it a natural for pots that can be moved indoors when cold threatens, though it still wants steady feeding and water. Harvest the small, sweet fingers when they plump and pale.

planting.note: Plant in spring; this compact mutation often throws two bunches per stem.

directions: Plant the corm just below grade in rich, well-drained soil in full sun. This Dwarf Cavendish sport can produce two flower stalks from a single pseudostem, so feed and water heavily to support the extra fruit. Harvest each bunch when the fingers round out and pale, ripening off the plant.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring after the last frost so roots establish before summer heat.

directions: Set the grafted tree so the graft union stays above the soil and plant on a mound if drainage is poor. This type A flower blooms and fruits heavily; pairing with a type B such as Fuerte or Bacon boosts set. Fruit ripens on the tree for months but softens only after picking, so harvest when the pebbly skin darkens and the flesh yields to gentle pressure a few days off the tree.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a protected spot, as young trees resent cold wind.

directions: Plant this spreading Mexican-Guatemalan hybrid with room to grow wide and stake it while young. It is a type B bloomer and makes an excellent pollinizer for Hass. Harvest the smooth green pear-shaped fruit in winter and early spring once it sizes up, then let it soften indoors.

planting.note: Plant in spring; this cold-tolerant variety suits cooler coastal gardens.

directions: Give this upright, relatively hardy tree a sunny sheltered site and mound the roots for drainage. As a type B flower it pollinates type A varieties like Hass well. Pick the thin-skinned green fruit in fall and early winter when it reaches full size, and ripen off the tree.

planting.note: Plant in spring; tolerates cool conditions better than most.

directions: Plant this vigorous, upright, cold-tolerant tree with good drainage and steady moisture. It is a reliable type B pollinizer often grown alongside Hass. Harvest the light green, pear-shaped fruit in fall and early winter when fully sized, then soften indoors.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm location for this Guatemalan-type variety.

directions: This upright, compact tree fits smaller yards; plant it high on a mound and water deeply in dry spells. A type A bloomer, it sets fruit well even on its own. Harvest the large, round, thick-skinned green fruit through summer when it reaches full size, and ripen off the tree.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, sheltered site.

directions: Plant this spreading, productive tree with room to widen and keep the crown mounded for drainage. It is a type A flower and benefits from a nearby type B pollinizer. Harvest the elongated, small-seeded green fruit from winter into spring when fully grown, then soften indoors.

planting.note: Plant in spring; its compact size suits smaller gardens and containers.

directions: This dense, semi-dwarf Hass-like tree stays smaller and can be grown in a large container. Plant on a mound and pair with a type B for best set, as it is a type A bloomer. Harvest the thick-skinned green fruit through spring and summer when full-sized, and ripen off the tree.

planting.note: Plant in spring; this compact, heat-tolerant tree handles inland heat.

directions: Plant this upright, compact Hass relative on a well-drained mound; it tolerates heat and sun better than standard Hass. It is a type A bloomer and fruits well with a type B nearby. Harvest the dark, pebbly fruit in summer and fall when it reaches full size, then let it soften indoors.

planting.note: Plant in spring; among the most cold-hardy avocados for marginal areas.

directions: This vigorous Mexican-race tree tolerates cold into the high teens once established; plant it in spring with excellent drainage. Leaves have an anise scent when crushed and it is a type A bloomer. Harvest the small, thin, glossy black fruit in fall when the skin darkens fully, and eat soon after softening.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a hardy Mexican type for cooler climates.

directions: A larger-fruited selection of Mexicola with the same strong cold tolerance; plant it high on a mound with good drainage. It is a type A flower and reliably productive in warm-summer areas. Harvest the glossy dark fruit in fall when fully colored, then ripen indoors.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a hardy Mexican type suited to cooler gardens.

directions: Plant this cold-hardy, relatively compact Mexican-race tree in spring with fast drainage. It is a type A bloomer that fruits at a young age. Harvest the small, thin-skinned black fruit in fall when the skin darkens, and enjoy soon after it softens.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, sheltered position.

directions: This Hass-like variety has flesh that resists browning after cutting; plant it on a well-drained mound. It is a type B bloomer and pairs nicely with type A trees like Hass. Harvest the dark, thick-skinned fruit in winter and spring when full-sized, then soften indoors.

planting.note: Plant in spring; its dwarf, weeping habit suits containers and patios.

directions: A dwarf, spreading tree ideal for large pots; plant in fast-draining mix and protect from frost. It is a type A bloomer that can set fruit on its own. Harvest the large green fruit in late summer and fall when full-sized, and ripen off the tree.

planting.note: Plant in spring; the only true dwarf avocado, well suited to containers.

directions: This compact, weeping dwarf stays small enough for pots and small yards; plant in well-drained mix and stake lightly. Both flower types are present so it fruits without a partner. Harvest the medium green pear-shaped fruit in summer when fully sized, then soften indoors.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, humid site; popular in Hawaii.

directions: A Guatemalan-Mexican hybrid prized in Hawaii for its rich, nutty flesh; plant in spring with excellent drainage. It is a type B bloomer and benefits from a type A nearby. Harvest the green, slightly rough-skinned fruit in winter when full-sized, then ripen off the tree.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm site for this Guatemalan-race tree.

directions: Plant this large, rounded Guatemalan tree with plenty of room and mound the roots. It is a type B bloomer that tends toward alternate bearing. Harvest the nearly round, thick-skinned green fruit in summer when full-sized, and soften indoors.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a Florida favorite for warm, humid climates.

directions: This vigorous West Indian-Guatemalan hybrid produces very large, smooth green fruit; plant it in spring with room to spread. It is a type A bloomer well suited to Florida conditions. Harvest in fall and early winter when the fruit reaches full size, then ripen off the tree.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a productive Florida variety for humid climates.

directions: A large, firm-fleshed Florida hybrid; plant in spring with good drainage and space to grow. It is a type B bloomer that holds up well when cut. Harvest the large green fruit in late fall and winter once full-sized, and soften indoors.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a classic Florida variety often used as rootstock.

directions: This dependable Florida hybrid tolerates a touch more cold than most West Indian types; plant in spring with good drainage. It is a type A bloomer and pollinates well with type B trees. Harvest the pear-shaped green fruit in fall and early winter when full-sized, then ripen off the tree.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a cold-tolerant Florida variety with purple fruit.

directions: An unusually cold-tolerant Florida hybrid with dark purple, thin skin; plant on a well-drained mound. It is a type B bloomer and fairly compact. Harvest the smooth purple-black fruit in summer and fall when fully colored and sized, then soften indoors.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a frost-free, humid location.

directions: A large-fruited West Indian variety for truly tropical gardens; plant in spring with excellent drainage. It is a type A bloomer and best paired with a type B. Harvest the big, smooth green fruit in summer when full-sized, then ripen off the tree.

planting.note: Plant in spring; an early-season West Indian variety from Florida.

directions: An old West Indian standard that ripens early in the season; plant in spring in a frost-free site. It is a type A bloomer and vigorous in tropical heat. Harvest the medium-large green fruit in late summer and fall when full-sized, then soften indoors.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a large-fruited West Indian variety for the tropics.

directions: This West Indian variety yields very large, mild fruit; plant in spring with good drainage in a frost-free spot. It is a type B bloomer that fruits best with a type A partner. Harvest the big green fruit in summer and early fall when full-sized, then ripen off the tree.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a productive late-season Florida hybrid.

directions: A reliable West Indian-Guatemalan hybrid that bears heavily; plant in spring with room to spread. It is a type B bloomer and pairs well with type A trees. Harvest the round-oval green fruit in late fall when full-sized, then soften indoors.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a hardy Florida variety for cooler subtropical areas.

directions: A vigorous, cold-hardy hybrid that fruits into winter; plant in spring with good drainage. It is a type B bloomer and dependable in marginal climates. Harvest the large green fruit from late fall through winter once full-sized, then ripen off the tree.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a compact, cold-tolerant variety that fruits young.

directions: This compact, precocious variety begins bearing at a young age; plant in spring on a well-drained mound. It is a type B bloomer that sets fruit reliably. Harvest the green pear-shaped fruit in fall when full-sized, then soften indoors.

planting.note: Transplant into warm ground once nights stay reliably mild.

directions: Start this productive hybrid from seed in warmth and set it out as a young plant to avoid root disturbance. It is self-pollinating and begins bearing low on the trunk while still short, so fruit stays within easy reach. Harvest the elongated red-fleshed fruit when the rind turns mostly yellow.

planting.note: Move seedlings outdoors after the last frost when soil is warm.

directions: Germinate seed in a warm tray and pot up before planting into fast-draining soil. This Hawaiian Solo type yields small, pear-shaped fruit with sweet salmon-pink flesh and is largely self-fertile. Cut fruit when the skin has colored on at least a quarter of its surface and finish ripening indoors.

planting.note: Plant out in late spring once the ground has warmed fully.

directions: Sow the flat black seeds shallowly and keep them warm and evenly moist until germination. This large-fruited Mexican type produces heavy oval fruit with firm reddish flesh on a vigorous plant. Harvest when the rind shifts from green to golden and the flesh gives slightly at the blossom end.

planting.note: Set out after frost when soil temperatures are consistently warm.

directions: Raise seedlings in warmth and transplant carefully into rich, well-drained soil. This low-bearing Solo selection sets glossy round fruit close to the ground with thick, sweet orange flesh. Pick as the skin colors up and softens near the tip.

planting.note: Transplant to a warm, sheltered spot once frost has passed.

directions: Start this vigorous Taiwanese hybrid from seed in warm conditions and pot on before planting out. It bears long, heavy fruit with reddish-orange flesh and adapts well to varied tropical soils. Harvest when the rind turns yellow and the fruit softens slightly under the fingers.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring once frost danger has passed.

directions: Plant a grafted tree in full sun and keep it watered while new growth pushes. This selection bears large fruit with sweet pink flesh and few seeds, and responds well to tip pruning to stay compact. Harvest when the fruit gives to gentle pressure and perfumes the air.

planting.note: Establish in spring for a full season of warm-weather growth.

directions: Plant in a warm, protected spot with rich, well-drained soil and water regularly. This variety yields tender fruit with creamy white, aromatic flesh that is mild and very sweet. Pick when the skin softens and turns pale yellow.

planting.note: Plant after frost so the tree roots into warming soil.

directions: Set the tree in full sun and mulch well to conserve moisture. This Hawaiian selection is a heavy, reliable bearer of round pink-fleshed fruit prized for juice and processing. Harvest when fully colored and slightly soft, as fruit ripens over an extended season.

planting.note: Plant out in spring in a warm, sheltered site.

directions: Plant this yellow-fruited form of the cattley guava in full sun and well-drained soil. It grows as a compact evergreen shrub and tolerates container culture and periodic light frost once established. Harvest the small round yellow fruit when soft and fragrant, with a bright, slightly tart flavor.

planting.note: Plant grafted or layered stock in spring after frost.

directions: Plant this popular Indian variety in deep, fertile soil and water consistently for the first seasons. It bears round white-fleshed fruit with a strong aroma and firm texture on a spreading tree. Harvest when the skin turns pale yellow-green and the fruit softens slightly.

planting.note: Plant in spring; established shrubs tolerate frost into the mid-teens.

directions: Set this evergreen shrub in full sun to partial shade in well-drained soil and water through dry spells. Many seedlings need a second plant for cross-pollination, so grow two unless you have a self-fertile named type. Let the gray-green fruit drop naturally, then gather it from the ground when fully ripe and fragrant.

planting.note: Plant in spring and pair with another variety for best set.

directions: Plant this large-fruited New Zealand variety in a sunny, well-drained spot. It is partly self-fertile but crops far more heavily with a second feijoa nearby for cross-pollination. Harvest the big oval fruit when it drops and the interior has turned soft and fragrant.

planting.note: Plant in spring; self-fertile but improved by a pollinizer.

directions: Grow this self-fruitful selection in full sun and give it steady water while fruit develops. It produces large, pear-shaped fruit with pale, smooth flesh and mild, sweet flavor. Let the fruit fall to gather at peak ripeness, or pick when it releases with a light twist.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny, sheltered location.

directions: Plant this New Zealand variety in full sun with good drainage and mulch to keep roots cool. It is largely self-fertile and yields large fruit with smooth-textured, sweet flesh. Harvest as fruit drops or gives easily, then let it soften a day or two if picked slightly early.

planting.note: Plant in spring and grow with a compatible pollinizer for full crops.

directions: Set this firm-fruited variety in full sun and well-drained ground. It needs cross-pollination for heavy set, so plant it near another feijoa such as Apollo. Gather the oval fruit when it falls and the gritty-sweet flesh has ripened through.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring or fall in mild-winter areas.

directions: Plant a grafted tree in full sun and thin the fruit clusters to improve size and quality. This classic California variety bears yellow-skinned fruit with juicy, sweet-tart white flesh. Harvest when the skin deepens to gold and the fruit softens on the branch.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall where winters stay mild.

directions: Set the tree in full sun and prune lightly after harvest to keep it manageable. This late-ripening variety produces large, round orange fruit with firm, sweet flesh that stores well. Pick when fully colored and yielding, usually later in the season than most loquats.

planting.note: Plant grafted stock in spring or fall in a frost-protected spot.

directions: Plant in full sun with good drainage and thin clusters for the largest fruit. This variety is known for big, yellow-orange fruit with sweet, slightly tangy flesh. Harvest when the skin is fully colored and the fruit softens to the touch.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in mild-winter regions.

directions: Plant this early-ripening variety in full sun and water steadily as fruit forms. It produces attractive orange-red fruit with sweet flesh ahead of most other loquats. Pick when fully colored and soft, harvesting over several passes as fruit ripens.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in a sheltered, sunny location.

directions: Grow this variety in full sun and thin the fruit for best flavor and size. It bears pale-skinned fruit with sweet, mild white flesh that many consider among the finest for fresh eating. Harvest when the skin turns creamy and the fruit softens on the tree.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring or fall; a late, cold-tolerant type.

directions: Plant this vigorous variety in full sun with well-drained soil. It is a late-ripening type that produces large, orange, sweet-tart fruit and tolerates cooler conditions than many loquats. Pick when fully colored and softened, usually toward the end of the loquat season.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring; more compact than seedlings.

directions: Plant this popular self-fertile variety in full sun and well-drained soil. It stays smaller than a seedling tree and bears over a long season, so thin and prune to manage the heavy crop. Harvest when the fruit softens slightly, then ripen fully off the tree before eating.

planting.note: Plant grafted stock in spring in a sunny, protected spot.

directions: Set this vigorous variety in full sun with deep, well-drained soil and stake young trees against wind. It bears large, sweet fruit reliably and benefits from summer pruning to limit its size. Pick when the fruit gives to light pressure and let it soften completely indoors.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm site with good drainage.

directions: Plant this variety in full sun with room to grow, as it makes a large and productive tree. It yields big fruit with rich, custard-like flesh and a sweet flavor. Harvest when firm-ripe and yielding, then let the fruit soften fully before eating.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring in a sheltered, sunny location.

directions: Grow this variety in full sun and well-drained soil, pruning to keep its vigorous growth in check. It produces smooth-skinned fruit with sweet, mild flesh over an extended season. Pick when the fruit begins to soften and ripen it the rest of the way indoors.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a somewhat more compact and productive selection.

directions: Plant this variety in full sun with deep, well-drained soil and water regularly through dry spells. It is a heavy bearer of sweet, aromatic fruit and stays more manageable than many seedlings. Harvest when the fruit yields gently, then allow it to soften fully for the best flavor.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring after frost danger passes in a wind-sheltered site.

directions: Set the grafted tree with its union above the soil line and stake it while young. This Spanish selection sets heavily with hand-pollination, so transfer pollen in the evening for smooth, well-filled fruit. Pick when the skin pales to yellow-green and the flesh gives to light pressure, then ripen off the tree.

planting.note: Plant in spring once frosts end; protect young trees from cold snaps.

directions: Plant the grafted tree in fast-draining soil and water deeply but infrequently once established. This vigorous cultivar carries a lightly tangy, papaya-toned flesh and responds well to evening hand-pollination for fuller fruit. Harvest when the fruit softens slightly and lightens in color, finishing the ripening indoors.

planting.note: Set out grafted trees in spring in a warm, sheltered location.

directions: Give this reliable California selection full sun, sharp drainage, and shelter from drying wind. Hand-pollinate flowers in the evening to counter poor natural set and improve fruit shape. Pick when the skin turns pale and the flesh yields to a gentle press, then ripen on the counter.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost, spacing well for a spreading canopy.

directions: Plant the grafted tree in well-drained ground and mulch to keep roots cool and evenly moist. Known for sweet, juicy, smooth-textured flesh, it benefits from evening hand-pollination for consistent fruit. Harvest as the fruit pales and softens slightly, letting it finish ripening indoors.

planting.note: Set out grafted trees in spring in a frost-free, sunny spot.

directions: Plant in loose, fast-draining soil and prune lightly in late winter to shape an open, easily worked canopy. This dependable cultivar sets better with hand-pollination applied in the evening. Pick fruit when it lightens and gives to gentle pressure, then soften indoors before eating.

planting.note: Plant in spring when weather settles and soil is warm.

directions: Give this large-fruited, bumpy-skinned selection full sun and protection from cold winds. Hand-pollinate in the evening for well-shaped fruit and thin heavy sets to reduce limb strain. Harvest when the fruit pales and softens slightly, ripening the rest of the way indoors.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring in a frost-free, sheltered site.

directions: Plant this red- to mauve-skinned selection in full sun with fast-draining soil and shelter from wind. Hand-pollinate flowers in the evening to ensure fully segmented fruit. Harvest when the knobby segments loosen and pull apart slightly, then let the fruit finish softening indoors.

planting.note: Set out young trees in spring once frost risk has ended.

directions: This cherimoya-by-sugar-apple hybrid grows best in full sun with rich, well-drained soil. Because natural pollination is unreliable, transfer pollen by hand in the evening for smooth, well-filled fruit. Harvest when the fruit lightens in color and the segments begin to separate, ripening it indoors until soft.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring in a warm, sheltered location.

directions: This widely grown Israeli selection is prized for setting fruit more readily than most atemoyas. Plant in full sun with sharp drainage, and give an evening hand-pollination for the fullest, best-shaped fruit. Pick when the skin pales and the segments loosen slightly, then soften indoors.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost, spacing for a compact, spreading tree.

directions: A popular, early-bearing cultivar that stays fairly compact and fruits at a young age. Plant in full sun with well-drained soil and hand-pollinate in the evening to reduce lumpy, uneven fruit. Harvest when the skin lightens and gives slightly, finishing the ripening off the tree.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring in a frost-free, sunny site.

directions: This Australian favorite produces very large, high-quality fruit but needs reliable hand-pollination to set well. Plant in full sun with deep, well-drained soil and shelter from strong wind. Harvest when the fruit lightens and the segments start to loosen, ripening indoors until it softens.

planting.note: Plant in hot, humid, frost-free lowlands with ample space.

directions: This large-fruited tropical tree carries rusty-brown, spiny fruit and needs plenty of heat and room. Sow fresh seed half an inch deep in warm soil, allowing a month or more for slow germination. Harvest when the heavy fruit softens and the flesh turns fragrant and orange; ripen it off the tree.

planting.note: Set out air-layered trees in spring once nights stay reliably warm.

directions: Plant an air-layered tree in a wind-sheltered spot with the root ball level with the surrounding soil and mulch heavily, keeping mulch off the trunk. Water steadily through the growing season but ease off in late winter to encourage a strong bloom. Fruit clusters ripen bright red in early summer and are cut with a short section of stem attached.

planting.note: Set out in spring after any danger of cold has passed.

directions: Plant this cultivar in full sun with shelter from strong wind and keep the root zone evenly moist during fruit set. It is prized for large fruit with small, often shriveled seeds and a high proportion of sweet flesh. Harvest the heart-shaped, rosy fruit when fully colored in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring into deep, well-drained soil.

directions: Give this dark-leaved cultivar a warm, sheltered position and mulch to hold soil moisture during the dry season. Reduce watering briefly in late winter to help trigger flowering, then resume as fruit swells. The dark red fruit has firm, fragrant, crisp flesh and ripens in early summer.

planting.note: Set out air-layered trees in spring as the weather warms.

directions: Plant this Hawaiian selection in rich, well-drained soil and stake young trees until established. It tends to flower and fruit dependably in humid tropical climates where some other lychees are shy bearers. Large, deep red, elongated fruit ripens through early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, protected location.

directions: Plant a grafted tree in full sun and keep it evenly watered while young to build a strong framework. This Hawaiian cultivar is a consistent, early-bearing producer of sweet, brown-skinned fruit. Harvest the clustered fruit in late summer when the skin turns fully tan and the flesh is translucent and juicy.

planting.note: Set out in spring into deep, fertile, well-drained soil.

directions: Give this Thai cultivar full sun and steady moisture, easing back slightly before bloom to encourage flowering. Thin overloaded fruit clusters so the remaining fruit sizes well. Prized for thick, crisp, very sweet flesh, it ripens tan-skinned fruit in late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring once soil has warmed.

directions: Plant this precocious cultivar in a sunny, sheltered spot and support the young trunk against wind. It often begins bearing at a young age and can flower more than once a year in warm climates. Pick the sweet, tan fruit in clusters when fully colored, usually in summer.

planting.note: Plant in the warm, wet season into rich, moisture-retentive soil.

directions: Plant a grafted tree in fertile soil in a hot, humid, wind-protected site, as this species resents cold and drying wind. Keep the root zone consistently moist and mulched, since rambutan is sensitive to drought. This popular Thai cultivar bears bright red, hairy fruit with sweet flesh that pulls cleanly from the seed; harvest when fully red.

planting.note: Set out during warm, rainy weather in a frost-free site.

directions: Give this Malaysian cultivar deep, rich soil, abundant moisture, and shelter from cold snaps that quickly damage the tree. Mulch generously and feed regularly through the growing season to support heavy fruiting. The large red fruit has firm, sweet, freestone flesh and ripens in summer.

planting.note: Plant into warm, humid conditions during the rainy season.

directions: Plant this Malaysian selection in fertile, well-drained but moist soil in a sheltered, frost-free location. Maintain steady irrigation and mulch to keep the shallow roots cool and damp. The dark red fruit with sweet, aromatic flesh ripens over the summer months.

planting.note: Transplant into a warm, humid, wind-sheltered site during the wet season.

directions: Sow the fresh, recalcitrant seed promptly since it loses viability quickly, keeping the medium warm and constantly moist. Give young trees partial shade and high humidity for the first few years, moving them to more sun as they mature. This slow, demanding tree may take many years to bear its purple, snowy-fleshed fruit, which is picked when the rind turns deep purple.

planting.note: Plant in a warm, sheltered spot during the rainy season.

directions: Plant grafted trees in rich, moist, well-drained soil with light shade while young, as they naturally grow as understory trees. Keep them well watered and protected from wind and cold. This thick-skinned, nearly latex-free type carries clusters of sweet, translucent fruit that ripen tan to yellow in late summer.

planting.note: Plant in warm, humid, shaded ground during the wet season.

directions: Plant this short, spiny, clustering palm in rich, moist soil with dappled shade, mimicking its humid forest home. Grow female plants for fruit and keep a male nearby for pollination, or hand-pollinate the flowers. Reddish-brown, scaly fruit forms in clusters at the base and is peeled to reveal crisp, sweet-tart segments.

planting.note: Plant during warm, wet weather in a deep, sheltered site.

directions: Plant a grafted tree in deep, fertile, well-drained soil in a hot, humid, wind-protected location and keep it well irrigated. Support developing fruit is unnecessary, but ample water and feeding help the heavy pods size up. This popular Thai cultivar bears large, spiny fruit with thick, mild, sweet flesh; the fruit is usually harvested as it nears full maturity and allowed to finish ripening.

planting.note: Plant in the wet season into rich, deep, well-drained soil.

directions: Give this prized Malaysian cultivar a warm, humid, sheltered site with fertile soil and steady moisture. Grow from a grafted tree for true-to-type fruit and earlier bearing than seedlings. It is famed for deep golden, rich, bittersweet flesh, and ripe fruit typically drops from the tree when ready.

planting.note: Plant in spring into moist, fertile, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant in full sun in moisture-retentive soil, as this tree grows best with plenty of water during fruiting. Light pruning after harvest keeps it compact and encourages new flowering wood. The glossy, bell-shaped fruit is crisp and mildly sweet and is picked when fully colored and slightly waxy to the touch.

planting.note: Set out rooted cuttings once nights stay reliably above about 50F.

directions: Root a 12 to 18 inch stem cutting after letting the cut end callus for a week, then plant it beside a sturdy post or trellis for the climbing stems. Water when the top few inches of soil dry and feed lightly through the warm season, avoiding soggy roots. Fruit swells within about a month of the night-blooming white flowers and is ready when the skin turns fully pink and gives slightly.

planting.note: Plant rooted stems in spring into fast-draining, gritty soil.

directions: Grow from a callused cutting trained onto a post with a wide top frame so the trailing stems can drape and fruit. Give it warmth, good drainage, and occasional feeding; hand-pollinate the large white flowers in the evening to boost set. Harvest the crimson-skinned, magenta-fleshed fruit once the color is even and the wings soften.

planting.note: Plant rooted suckers or crowns of this near-spineless type in warm soil.

directions: This standard cannery variety has smooth leaf margins, so handling is easier than with spiny types. Root a crown or sucker, plant it shallowly, and feed lightly through the growing season for a large cylindrical fruit. It ripens over a year or more and is best cut when the shell yellows and the flesh turns fragrant and juicy.

planting.note: Plant in spring; established plants tolerate brief light chill.

directions: Grow from a grafted plant or fresh seed in full sun and average, well-drained soil. It is adaptable and drought-tolerant once established but fruits best with steady moisture and light pruning to keep it open. The round fruit is ripe when it softens, its skin lightens, and a musky sweet fragrance develops.

planting.note: Set vines on a trellis after frost once soil warms.

directions: Start seed in warm pots or plant a nursery vine at the base of a strong trellis or fence for the tendrils to climb. Give it full sun, steady water, and light feeding, and thin crowded growth for airflow. The round fruit is ripe when the skin colors fully and it drops, wrinkling slightly as the aromatic pulp sweetens.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in late spring as the ground warms.

directions: Set this reliable grafted selection with the graft union above the soil and stake it the first season. Feed lightly several times through the warm months and mulch to hold moisture. Fruit hangs long on the tree; pick when the skin scratches pink and ripen at room temperature until it yields to a gentle squeeze.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring in a sheltered, sunny spot.

directions: This compact, everbearing selection stays smaller than seedling trees and suits home gardens. Plant with the graft above soil, mulch, and water through dry periods. It fruits over an extended season; harvest when the skin gives to light pressure and finish ripening on the counter.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in warm weather in full sun.

directions: A dependable, heavy-bearing grafted selection that stays more manageable than seedlings. Plant with the graft above the soil line and water steadily while establishing. Harvest when a light scratch reveals brown flesh; ripen on the counter until the fruit is soft and malty-sweet.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in the warm season in full sun.

directions: The leading commercial sweet carambola, this grafted selection bears heavy crops of firm, orange fruit. Plant with the graft above the soil and shield from wind. Keep moisture steady and thin crowded fruit for size. Harvest when the ribs color deep yellow-orange, when it holds and ships well while staying sweet.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees during warm, frost-free weather.

directions: A very sweet Hawaiian selection prized for fresh eating and consistent bearing. Site it out of drying wind and keep the root zone mulched and moist. Feed lightly through the growing season. Harvest when the thick ribs turn bright golden and the flesh is juicy and low in acid.

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in warm weather in a sheltered, sunny spot.

directions: A large, pale, crisp Thai selection with mild sweetness even when picked early. Plant out of wind in fertile, well-drained soil and keep it evenly watered. Thin heavy sets for bigger fruit. Harvest when the broad ribs shade from green to light yellow; it eats well slightly firm.
